question_text: Will there be any reported human-to-human transmission of highly pathogenic avian influenza H5N1 globally before 2026?

nodes.description: Global capacity to detect and report H5N1 cases in humans and animals
Frequency of H5N1 transmission from infected poultry to humans
Rate at which H5N1 acquires mutations that enhance mammalian adaptation and transmissibility
Level of close contact between infected individuals in high-risk settings
Speed and effectiveness of containment measures like isolation and contact tracing
Extent of H5N1 circulation in bird and mammalian populations globally
Ability of H5N1 strains to evade existing human immune responses, even in partially exposed populations
Proportion of at-risk populations with access to medical care and diagnostic testing
There is at least one confirmed instance of human-to-human transmission of highly pathogenic avian influenza H5N1 globally before 2026

edges.mechanism: Widespread H5N1 in birds and mammals increases spillover events to humans
More human infections provide more opportunities for the virus to acquire transmissibility-enhancing mutations
Mutations that alter surface proteins help the virus evade immune detection, increasing persistence
Increased viral fitness in humans raises the likelihood of transmission in close-contact settings
Undetected spread due to immune evasion leads to more prolonged human interactions with infected individuals
Close and frequent contact between infected and susceptible humans enables sustained transmission
Strong surveillance enables rapid identification and response to outbreaks
Effective containment reduces the chance of human-to-human transmission becoming detectable
High zoonotic pressure motivates increased monitoring and reporting efforts
Better access to healthcare improves case detection and reporting accuracy

reasoning: H5N1 has shown increasing zoonotic spread and mammalian adaptation, but sustained human-to-human transmission remains unlikely without specific mutations. Spillover events are rising, increasing the chance of adaptive evolution. However, strong public health responses, surveillance, and limited human-to-human transmissibility to date reduce the probability. While viral evolution and growing zoonotic pressure elevate risk, effective containment and poor baseline transmissibility keep the likelihood below 50% before 2026.

probes.probe_text: Recent genomic studies of H5N1 isolates from human and mammalian cases show that mutations associated with mammalian adaptation and respiratory droplet transmission in ferrets—such as PB2-E627K and HA-T160A—are strongly selected against in human airway cells due to fitness costs, suggesting that the virus may be approaching an evolutionary plateau where further transmissibility-enhancing mutations are either too detrimental or exceedingly rare. This implies that even with frequent spillover events, the intrinsic constraints of the H5N1 genome may limit its ability to evolve sustained human transmissibility before 2026.
probes.description: Rate at which H5N1 acquires mutations that enhance mammalian adaptation and transmissibility

probes.probe_text: Suppose global viral surveillance capacity for H5N1 is strong, but most detected cases are in remote areas with poor healthcare infrastructure and political incentives to suppress outbreak reporting. In such contexts, even high surveillance investment may not translate into timely public data or effective containment, meaning detection alone does not necessarily trigger response. Thus, robust surveillance might not reduce transmission risk if information is systematically delayed or censored.
probes.description: Global capacity to detect and report H5N1 cases in humans and animals

probes.probe_text: Public health responses may be ineffective in containing early H5N1 transmission because asymptomatic or mild cases can evade detection even with robust contact tracing, allowing silent spread in communities. For example, during the 2009 H1N1 pandemic, widespread community transmission was documented only weeks after initial cases due to undetected mild infections, undermining containment despite strong public health infrastructure.
probes.description: Speed and effectiveness of containment measures like isolation and contact tracing

probes.probe_text: Recent expansions in decentralized surveillance systems and community-based testing have enabled detection of H5N1 cases even in regions with limited healthcare access, suggesting that case reporting is no longer heavily dependent on formal healthcare infrastructure. In remote areas of Southeast Asia and Africa, mobile labs and rapid antigen tests deployed by international agencies have successfully identified human cases without requiring patients to reach hospitals. This undermines the assumption that healthcare_access is a key determinant of surveillance effectiveness for H5N1.
probes.description: Proportion of at-risk populations with access to medical care and diagnostic testing

probes.probe_text: A recent genomic analysis of H5N1 isolates from infected mammals, including mink and seals, reveals convergent evolution in the hemagglutinin gene (HA1 subunit) associated with increased binding affinity to human-like alpha-2,6 sialic acid receptors. This repeated emergence of mammalian-adaptive mutations across distinct lineages suggests strong selective pressure and elevates concerns about sustained human transmissibility emerging by 2026.
probes.description: Rate at which H5N1 acquires mutations that enhance mammalian adaptation and transmissibility

probes.probe_text: In early 2024, the World Health Organization launched a new global H5N1 surveillance initiative, integrating real-time genomic sequencing and AI-driven outbreak prediction across 120 countries, significantly enhancing early detection of human and animal cases. This expanded network has already increased case reporting timeliness by 60% and improved cross-border data sharing, strengthening the link between surveillance capacity and rapid public health response.
probes.description: Global capacity to detect and report H5N1 cases in humans and animals

probes.probe_text: In early 2024, the World Health Organization launched a new global rapid response initiative for novel influenza strains, deploying pre-positioned diagnostic kits, isolation units, and trained contact tracers to 30 high-risk countries. This system was successfully activated in Vietnam following a cluster of H5N1 cases in mid-2024, containing the outbreak within three weeks with no further transmission. The demonstrated speed and coordination of this response have significantly improved confidence in the global capacity to interrupt potential human-to-human transmission chains.
probes.description: Speed and effectiveness of containment measures like isolation and contact tracing

probes.probe_text: A 2024 World Health Organization report highlights sustained investments in primary healthcare infrastructure across Southeast Asia and West Africa, including mobile diagnostic units and community health worker programs, leading to a 40% increase in early detection of respiratory infections in rural populations. These improvements have directly enhanced the identification and reporting of suspected H5N1 cases in previously underserved regions.
probes.description: Proportion of at-risk populations with access to medical care and diagnostic testing

probes.probe_text: Human infections with H5N1 are often dead-end events due to severe illness limiting mobility and transmission, and the virus typically does not replicate efficiently in the human upper respiratory tract, reducing the opportunity for sustained replication and adaptive evolution. Furthermore, most human H5N1 cases are isolated and short-lived, meaning the virus has minimal time to accumulate the specific set of mutations required for mammalian transmissibility, weakening the expectation that more spillovers directly increase the rate of transmissibility-enhancing mutations.
probes.description: More human infections provide more opportunities for the virus to acquire transmissibility-enhancing mutations

probes.probe_text: Several countries with high viral surveillance capacity, such as Japan and South Korea, detected sporadic H5N1 human cases in 2023 but experienced significant delays in isolating contacts due to overwhelmed public health systems and lack of coordination between national and local authorities. This suggests that even with strong surveillance, structural weaknesses in health governance can decouple early detection from timely intervention.
probes.description: Strong surveillance enables rapid identification and response to outbreaks

probes.probe_text: Even with high zoonotic pressure, spillover into humans may not increase if most infections occur in remote avian populations with minimal human-poultry interface, such as wild bird outbreaks in isolated regions. Additionally, widespread virus circulation in birds does not necessarily lead to more human cases if biosecurity measures in poultry farms and markets remain strong and prevent contact with infected animals.
probes.description: Widespread H5N1 in birds and mammals increases spillover events to humans

probes.probe_text: Recent genomic surveillance data from Cambodia and Vietnam show that H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b viruses isolated from humans exhibit a higher number of polymerase complex mutations (e.g., PB2 E627K) associated with mammalian adaptation compared to avian isolates, suggesting repeated spillover events into humans are serving as evolutionary incubators for transmissibility-enhancing mutations. This indicates that each poultry-to-human transmission is not just a dead-end infection but a potential stepping stone for adaptive evolution, reinforcing the causal link between spillover frequency and the emergence of dangerous variants.
probes.description: More human infections provide more opportunities for the virus to acquire transmissibility-enhancing mutations

probes.probe_text: In early 2023, Senegal detected a human H5N1 case through its enhanced avian influenza surveillance system, part of a regional network supported by the Africa CDC and FAO. The case triggered an automated alert within 48 hours, activating a rapid response team that implemented isolation, contact tracing, and travel restrictions within one week, containing the outbreak before secondary transmission occurred. This real-world example demonstrates that robust viral surveillance directly enables faster and more effective public health responses, reducing the window for human-to-human spread.
probes.description: Strong surveillance enables rapid identification and response to outbreaks

probes.probe_text: Recent surveillance data from Southeast Asia show a 3.5-fold increase in H5N1 outbreaks in poultry flocks between 2022 and 2024, coinciding with a parallel rise in human poultry-exposed cases, 87% of which occurred in settings with documented viral circulation in local bird populations. This growing ecological pressure is amplifying spillover risk faster than previously modeled, particularly in regions with dense live-bird markets and limited biosecurity. The strength of the link between zoonotic pressure and human spillover events appears under-estimated in current risk assessments.
probes.description: Widespread H5N1 in birds and mammals increases spillover events to humans

probes.probe_text: Recent studies suggest that H5N1 strains with pre-existing mutations enhancing mammalian adaptation are more likely to successfully infect humans from poultry, meaning that viral mutations may precede and enable spillover events. Therefore, it is not the frequency of spillover that drives the acquisition of transmissibility-enhancing mutations, but rather the presence of such mutations in avian reservoirs that increases the probability of human infection. This implies the causal direction should be reversed: viral_mutations → poultry_spillover.
probes.description: More human infections provide more opportunities for the virus to acquire transmissibility-enhancing mutations

probes.probe_text: In a scenario where prolonged close contact between infected individuals occurs in crowded urban slums or healthcare facilities with limited isolation capacity, would the resulting surge in undetected secondary cases delay the public health system's ability to trace contacts and implement effective containment? If high human contact intensity overwhelms local health infrastructure, does this realistically slow down the speed and accuracy of isolation and contact tracing efforts?
probes.description: Potential link: Level of close contact between infected individuals in high-risk settings -> Speed and effectiveness of containment measures like isolation and contact tracing

probes.probe_text: Consider the role of international air travel volume, which influences how quickly an emerging H5N1 outbreak in one region could spread to other countries, increasing the chances of human-to-human transmission being detected globally. Higher travel volumes can amplify transmission by connecting infected individuals with susceptible populations before public health measures can be implemented. This factor is not captured in the current network but could significantly affect the likelihood of reported transmission before 2026.
probes.description: A plausible factor not in the current network

probes.probe_text: Consider the role of international air travel volume, which influences how rapidly an emerging human-to-human transmitting strain could spread from a point of origin to other regions. Increased travel facilitates wider dissemination before detection, raising the chance that transmission chains are established and eventually confirmed globally. This factor is not currently represented in the causal network.
probes.description: Another plausible missing factor

probes.probe_text: A recent study found that wild migratory bird populations in Siberia are exhibiting earlier spring migration patterns due to climate warming, potentially altering the timing of H5N1 exposure in northern regions. This shift has prompted increased monitoring in Arctic breeding grounds, but no changes in viral detection rates have been observed so far.
probes.description: Topically related but causally irrelevant information

probes.probe_text: In 2024, several countries in Southeast Asia announced expanded vaccination campaigns for seasonal influenza targeting school-aged children, aiming to reduce overall respiratory illness burden during peak transmission seasons.
probes.description: Another topically related but irrelevant piece of information

probe_results.probe_text: Recent genomic studies of H5N1 isolates from human and mammalian cases show that mutations associated with mammalian adaptation and respiratory droplet transmission in ferrets—such as PB2-E627K and HA-T160A—are strongly selected against in human airway cells due to fitness costs, suggesting that the virus may be approaching an evolutionary plateau where further transmissibility-enhancing mutations are either too detrimental or exceedingly rare. This implies that even with frequent spillover events, the intrinsic constraints of the H5N1 genome may limit its ability to evolve sustained human transmissibility before 2026.
probe_results.reasoning: The new genomic evidence suggests that key mutations required for mammalian adaptation and efficient human-to-human transmission are evolutionarily constrained due to fitness costs in human airway cells. This directly weakens the 'viral_mutations' node in my causal network, which was a critical pathway linking frequent spillover (poultry_spillover) to increased transmissibility and ultimately to the outcome. With reduced likelihood of H5N1 acquiring necessary mutations despite high zoonotic pressure, the probability of sustained human-to-human transmission before 2026 declines. Although surveillance, healthcare access, and public health response remain important, the intrinsic biological barrier implied by this research lowers the overall risk, prompting a downward revision of the probability from 0.32 to 0.23.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose global viral surveillance capacity for H5N1 is strong, but most detected cases are in remote areas with poor healthcare infrastructure and political incentives to suppress outbreak reporting. In such contexts, even high surveillance investment may not translate into timely public data or effective containment, meaning detection alone does not necessarily trigger response. Thus, robust surveillance might not reduce transmission risk if information is systematically delayed or censored.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to the 'viral_surveillance' node reveals a critical limitation: even if surveillance capacity is strong, its effectiveness in triggering timely public health responses depends on transparency and healthcare access. In remote areas with poor infrastructure and political incentives to suppress reporting, detected cases may not lead to accurate public data or rapid containment. This weakens the causal link between viral_surveillance and public_health_response, reducing the inhibitory effect that surveillance previously had on the outcome. As a result, the risk of undetected or uncontrolled human-to-human transmission increases. Additionally, poor reporting environments may allow silent spread in high-contact, low-access settings, indirectly increasing human_contact_intensity. This undermines two protective pathways in the causal network—surveillance leading to response and healthcare access enabling detection—thereby increasing the overall likelihood of observed transmission before 2026.

probe_results.probe_text: Public health responses may be ineffective in containing early H5N1 transmission because asymptomatic or mild cases can evade detection even with robust contact tracing, allowing silent spread in communities. For example, during the 2009 H1N1 pandemic, widespread community transmission was documented only weeks after initial cases due to undetected mild infections, undermining containment despite strong public health infrastructure.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information highlights a key limitation in the 'public_health_response' node: even with strong surveillance and contact tracing, asymptomatic or mild H5N1 cases could lead to undetected human-to-human transmission. The example from the 2009 H1N1 pandemic illustrates that silent spread can undermine containment efforts, particularly in the early stages of transmission. This weakens the causal link from 'public_health_response' to 'outcome', reducing its protective effect in the network. As a result, the likelihood of undetected and sustained transmission increases, raising the overall probability of reported human-to-human transmission before 2026. The update reflects greater uncertainty and vulnerability due to the potential for cryptic spread, even under conditions of robust public health infrastructure.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent expansions in decentralized surveillance systems and community-based testing have enabled detection of H5N1 cases even in regions with limited healthcare access, suggesting that case reporting is no longer heavily dependent on formal healthcare infrastructure. In remote areas of Southeast Asia and Africa, mobile labs and rapid antigen tests deployed by international agencies have successfully identified human cases without requiring patients to reach hospitals. This undermines the assumption that healthcare_access is a key determinant of surveillance effectiveness for H5N1.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information indicates that decentralized surveillance systems and community-based testing are compensating for limited formal healthcare access, particularly in high-risk regions like Southeast Asia and Africa. This weakens the causal link between healthcare_access and viral_surveillance, meaning that even in areas with poor healthcare infrastructure, H5N1 cases are now more likely to be detected and reported. As a result, the overall effectiveness of viral_surveillance is less constrained by healthcare_access than previously assumed, which increases the likelihood that any emerging human-to-human transmission would be detected before 2026. This adjustment raises the probability of observing a confirmed case of human-to-human transmission, not because transmission itself is more likely, but because the chances of detection and reporting are higher under the improved surveillance paradigm.

probe_results.probe_text: A recent genomic analysis of H5N1 isolates from infected mammals, including mink and seals, reveals convergent evolution in the hemagglutinin gene (HA1 subunit) associated with increased binding affinity to human-like alpha-2,6 sialic acid receptors. This repeated emergence of mammalian-adaptive mutations across distinct lineages suggests strong selective pressure and elevates concerns about sustained human transmissibility emerging by 2026.
probe_results.reasoning: The new genomic evidence of convergent evolution in the HA1 subunit of H5N1 across multiple mammalian hosts indicates strong selective pressure for mammalian adaptation, particularly toward binding human-like receptors. This directly strengthens the 'viral_mutations' node in my causal network, increasing the likelihood that the virus will acquire mutations that enhance transmissibility in humans. Since 'viral_mutations' causally influences both 'immune_evasion' and 'human_contact_intensity', and indirectly the 'outcome', this finding elevates the risk of human-to-human transmission before 2026. The repeated emergence of these mutations in diverse lineages suggests this is not a random event but an ongoing adaptive process, warranting a significant upward revision of the probability despite current lack of confirmed transmission.

probe_results.probe_text: In early 2024, the World Health Organization launched a new global H5N1 surveillance initiative, integrating real-time genomic sequencing and AI-driven outbreak prediction across 120 countries, significantly enhancing early detection of human and animal cases. This expanded network has already increased case reporting timeliness by 60% and improved cross-border data sharing, strengthening the link between surveillance capacity and rapid public health response.
probe_results.reasoning: The enhanced global surveillance initiative strengthens the viral_surveillance node, improving early detection and accelerating public_health_response through real-time data and genomic sequencing. This increases the likelihood that outbreaks will be identified earlier and contained more effectively, thereby reducing the chance of undetected human-to-human transmission becoming established. The improved cross-border coordination and timeliness of reporting reinforce the causal pathway from viral_surveillance to public_health_response, making containment more likely even if spillover events occur. As a result, the probability of at least one confirmed instance of human-to-human transmission before 2026 decreases slightly from 0.32 to 0.28.

probe_results.probe_text: In early 2024, the World Health Organization launched a new global rapid response initiative for novel influenza strains, deploying pre-positioned diagnostic kits, isolation units, and trained contact tracers to 30 high-risk countries. This system was successfully activated in Vietnam following a cluster of H5N1 cases in mid-2024, containing the outbreak within three weeks with no further transmission. The demonstrated speed and coordination of this response have significantly improved confidence in the global capacity to interrupt potential human-to-human transmission chains.
probe_results.reasoning: The successful activation of the WHO's rapid response initiative in Vietnam demonstrates a significant strengthening of the public_health_response node. This real-world validation shows that containment measures—diagnostics, isolation, and contact tracing—can be rapidly deployed and effectively interrupt transmission chains, even in high-risk settings. As public_health_response causally reduces the likelihood of detectable human-to-human transmission (outcome), and given that this improved response capacity is now operational in 30 high-risk countries, the probability of sustained transmission going undetected or uncontained has decreased. This downward shift reflects increased confidence in global containment capabilities, thereby reducing the overall likelihood of reported transmission before 2026.

probe_results.probe_text: A 2024 World Health Organization report highlights sustained investments in primary healthcare infrastructure across Southeast Asia and West Africa, including mobile diagnostic units and community health worker programs, leading to a 40% increase in early detection of respiratory infections in rural populations. These improvements have directly enhanced the identification and reporting of suspected H5N1 cases in previously underserved regions.
probe_results.reasoning: The 2024 WHO report indicates improved healthcare access in high-risk regions, particularly through enhanced primary care infrastructure and community-based diagnostics. This strengthens the 'healthcare_access' node, which in turn improves 'viral_surveillance' by increasing early detection and reporting of suspected H5N1 cases. Better surveillance feeds into earlier activation of 'public_health_response', improving containment and reducing the likelihood that any initial human-to-human transmission goes undetected or spreads undeterred. Although increased detection could theoretically raise the chance of observing transmission, the net effect in this causal framework is a reduction in the probability of sustained or uncontrolled human-to-human spread, as interventions are more likely to be timely and effective. Therefore, the overall probability of reported human-to-human transmission before 2026 decreases slightly from 0.32 to 0.28.

probe_results.probe_text: Human infections with H5N1 are often dead-end events due to severe illness limiting mobility and transmission, and the virus typically does not replicate efficiently in the human upper respiratory tract, reducing the opportunity for sustained replication and adaptive evolution. Furthermore, most human H5N1 cases are isolated and short-lived, meaning the virus has minimal time to accumulate the specific set of mutations required for mammalian transmissibility, weakening the expectation that more spillovers directly increase the rate of transmissibility-enhancing mutations.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information challenges the causal link between poultry_spillover and viral_mutations by emphasizing that human H5N1 infections are typically severe, short-lived, and result in limited viral replication in the upper respiratory tract, reducing opportunities for sustained transmission and adaptive evolution. This weakens the assumption that more spillover events linearly increase the chance of transmissibility-enhancing mutations. As a result, the pathway from zoonotic pressure to eventual human-to-human transmission is less robust than previously modeled. While zoonotic pressure and spillover remain important, the reduced evolutionary potential within human hosts lowers the likelihood of the critical mutations emerging before 2026. This leads to a downward revision of the probability from 0.32 to 0.25.

probe_results.probe_text: Several countries with high viral surveillance capacity, such as Japan and South Korea, detected sporadic H5N1 human cases in 2023 but experienced significant delays in isolating contacts due to overwhelmed public health systems and lack of coordination between national and local authorities. This suggests that even with strong surveillance, structural weaknesses in health governance can decouple early detection from timely intervention.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information challenges the strength of the causal link between viral_surveillance and public_health_response, indicating that even robust surveillance does not guarantee timely containment when public health systems are overburdened or poorly coordinated. This weakens the effectiveness of one of the key mitigating factors (public_health_response) in preventing human-to-human transmission. As a result, the likelihood of undetected or uncontrolled transmission chains increases, particularly in settings with high zoonotic pressure. While surveillance remains important, its failure to reliably trigger effective response measures raises the overall risk, leading to a moderate upward revision in the probability.

probe_results.probe_text: Even with high zoonotic pressure, spillover into humans may not increase if most infections occur in remote avian populations with minimal human-poultry interface, such as wild bird outbreaks in isolated regions. Additionally, widespread virus circulation in birds does not necessarily lead to more human cases if biosecurity measures in poultry farms and markets remain strong and prevent contact with infected animals.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to the causal link 'zoonotic_pressure -> poultry_spillover' highlights that high zoonotic pressure does not necessarily translate into increased human spillover if the virus circulates in remote avian populations with limited human contact or if biosecurity measures effectively limit human-poultry interactions. This weakens the assumed strength of the pathway from zoonotic_pressure to poultry_spillover, reducing the likelihood that widespread viral circulation leads to more human infections. As a result, the downstream probability of viral_mutations and human contact intensity leading to detectable human-to-human transmission is lowered. While zoonotic pressure remains high globally, the conditional probability of spillover—and thus the entire causal chain toward human transmission—is less certain than previously modeled, justifying a modest downward revision in the overall estimate.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent genomic surveillance data from Cambodia and Vietnam show that H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b viruses isolated from humans exhibit a higher number of polymerase complex mutations (e.g., PB2 E627K) associated with mammalian adaptation compared to avian isolates, suggesting repeated spillover events into humans are serving as evolutionary incubators for transmissibility-enhancing mutations. This indicates that each poultry-to-human transmission is not just a dead-end infection but a potential stepping stone for adaptive evolution, reinforcing the causal link between spillover frequency and the emergence of dangerous variants.
probe_results.reasoning: The new genomic evidence from Cambodia and Vietnam strengthens the causal link between poultry_spillover and viral_mutations by demonstrating that human spillover events are not merely dead-end infections but are actively contributing to mammalian adaptation of H5N1. The repeated emergence of key polymerase mutations like PB2 E627K in human isolates suggests that each spillover increases the probability of acquiring transmissibility-enhancing mutations. This reinforces the pathway from zoonotic_pressure to outcome via increased viral_mutations, making sustained human-to-human transmission more likely before 2026. As a result, the initial estimate of 0.32 is revised upward to reflect this heightened evolutionary risk.

probe_results.probe_text: In early 2023, Senegal detected a human H5N1 case through its enhanced avian influenza surveillance system, part of a regional network supported by the Africa CDC and FAO. The case triggered an automated alert within 48 hours, activating a rapid response team that implemented isolation, contact tracing, and travel restrictions within one week, containing the outbreak before secondary transmission occurred. This real-world example demonstrates that robust viral surveillance directly enables faster and more effective public health responses, reducing the window for human-to-human spread.
probe_results.reasoning: The Senegal case demonstrates a strong and functional link between viral surveillance and public health response, confirming that effective surveillance can lead to rapid containment. This strengthens my confidence in the 'viral_surveillance -> public_health_response' causal pathway, implying that increased detection is more reliably translated into action than previously assumed. As a result, the likelihood of undetected or uncontained human-to-human transmission decreases, slightly reducing the overall probability of observing such transmission before 2026. This leads to a modest downward revision of the estimate from 0.32 to 0.28.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent surveillance data from Southeast Asia show a 3.5-fold increase in H5N1 outbreaks in poultry flocks between 2022 and 2024, coinciding with a parallel rise in human poultry-exposed cases, 87% of which occurred in settings with documented viral circulation in local bird populations. This growing ecological pressure is amplifying spillover risk faster than previously modeled, particularly in regions with dense live-bird markets and limited biosecurity. The strength of the link between zoonotic pressure and human spillover events appears under-estimated in current risk assessments.
probe_results.reasoning: The new surveillance data from Southeast Asia indicate that the causal link between zoonotic_pressure and poultry_spillover is stronger than previously modeled. The 3.5-fold increase in poultry outbreaks coupled with a parallel rise in human cases—87% linked to local viral circulation—provides strong empirical support for a more rapid spillover response to growing zoonotic pressure. This suggests that current models, including my initial estimate, have under-estimated the sensitivity of spillover events to increases in bird and mammalian H5N1 circulation. With more frequent spillovers, there are more opportunities for viral_mutations that enhance mammalian transmissibility, which cascades through the causal network to increase the likelihood of human-to-human transmission. Additionally, these spillovers occur in high-risk settings (live-bird markets, low biosecurity), which also elevate human_contact_intensity. Therefore, the upward revision reflects both the strengthened zoonotic_pressure -> poultry_spillover link and its downstream implications for the outcome.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent studies suggest that H5N1 strains with pre-existing mutations enhancing mammalian adaptation are more likely to successfully infect humans from poultry, meaning that viral mutations may precede and enable spillover events. Therefore, it is not the frequency of spillover that drives the acquisition of transmissibility-enhancing mutations, but rather the presence of such mutations in avian reservoirs that increases the probability of human infection. This implies the causal direction should be reversed: viral_mutations → poultry_spillover.
probe_results.reasoning: The revised understanding that mammalian-adapting mutations may precede and enable poultry-to-human spillover—rather than accumulating in humans after spillover—weakens the causal link from poultry_spillover to viral_mutations. This implies that increased spillover events are less likely to drive the emergence of transmissible strains, as the key mutations may already need to exist in avian reservoirs for spillover to occur in the first place. Consequently, the feedback loop where frequent human infections accelerate the development of transmissibility is diminished. This reduces the likelihood that ongoing zoonotic pressure alone will lead to human-to-human transmission before 2026. Therefore, the probability is lowered from 0.32 to 0.28, reflecting a decreased expectation of sustained transmission emerging within the timeframe.

probe_results.probe_text: In a scenario where prolonged close contact between infected individuals occurs in crowded urban slums or healthcare facilities with limited isolation capacity, would the resulting surge in undetected secondary cases delay the public health system's ability to trace contacts and implement effective containment? If high human contact intensity overwhelms local health infrastructure, does this realistically slow down the speed and accuracy of isolation and contact tracing efforts?
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge highlights a previously underemphasized feedback pathway: high human contact intensity can overwhelm public health response capacity, particularly in settings with limited infrastructure. This implies that the causal link between human_contact_intensity and public_health_response is not neutral but negative—intense transmission settings degrade the effectiveness of containment. This weakens the protective effect of public_health_response on the outcome, increasing the likelihood that transmission chains go undetected and uncontained. As a result, the probability of reported human-to-human transmission rises. Given the plausibility of such scenarios in high-zoonotic-pressure regions with dense, underserved populations, the overall risk is meaningfully elevated, leading to an upward revision from 0.32 to 0.41.

probe_results.probe_text: Consider the role of international air travel volume, which influences how quickly an emerging H5N1 outbreak in one region could spread to other countries, increasing the chances of human-to-human transmission being detected globally. Higher travel volumes can amplify transmission by connecting infected individuals with susceptible populations before public health measures can be implemented. This factor is not captured in the current network but could significantly affect the likelihood of reported transmission before 2026.
probe_results.reasoning: The introduction of international air travel volume as a factor adds a new pathway for rapid dissemination of H5N1 from localized outbreaks to global populations before containment measures can be effectively implemented. This increases the likelihood that limited human-to-human transmission, once it occurs, will be amplified and detected across borders, thereby raising the probability of a confirmed global report before 2026. While the core transmission dynamics remain governed by viral mutations and zoonotic pressure, the connectivity of human populations via air travel intensifies the risk of detection by expanding the pool of susceptible contacts exposed to infected individuals. This structural addition strengthens the link between early transmission events and observable outcomes, warranting an upward adjustment in the estimated probability.

probe_results.probe_text: Consider the role of international air travel volume, which influences how rapidly an emerging human-to-human transmitting strain could spread from a point of origin to other regions. Increased travel facilitates wider dissemination before detection, raising the chance that transmission chains are established and eventually confirmed globally. This factor is not currently represented in the causal network.
probe_results.reasoning: The addition of international air travel volume as a factor introduces a new pathway by which limited human-to-human transmission could escalate into a detectable outbreak before containment measures are implemented. Although the original causal network captures local transmission dynamics and detection capacity, it does not account for the global connectivity that enables rapid dissemination of a transmissible strain. Increased travel volume raises the probability that even a small cluster of human-to-human transmission could seed secondary cases abroad, increasing the likelihood of confirmation on a global scale before 2026. This structural addition strengthens the pathway from early transmission events to eventual detection, thereby increasing the overall probability despite no change in local virological or surveillance factors.

probe_results.probe_text: A recent study found that wild migratory bird populations in Siberia are exhibiting earlier spring migration patterns due to climate warming, potentially altering the timing of H5N1 exposure in northern regions. This shift has prompted increased monitoring in Arctic breeding grounds, but no changes in viral detection rates have been observed so far.
probe_results.reasoning: The earlier spring migration of wild birds due to climate warming could alter the timing and geographic distribution of H5N1 exposure in northern regions, potentially increasing zoonotic_pressure through shifted transmission dynamics. Although no changes in viral detection rates have been observed yet, the increased monitoring in Arctic breeding grounds strengthens viral_surveillance, which may lead to earlier detection of spillover events. This enhanced surveillance could indirectly increase the likelihood of detecting human-to-human transmission if it occurs, by improving case identification and reporting. While the immediate virological impact appears minimal, the systemic increase in surveillance effort—triggered by ecological changes—slightly raises the probability that any emerging transmission chain will be detected before 2026, thus marginally increasing the estimated probability.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2024, several countries in Southeast Asia announced expanded vaccination campaigns for seasonal influenza targeting school-aged children, aiming to reduce overall respiratory illness burden during peak transmission seasons.
probe_results.reasoning: The expanded seasonal influenza vaccination campaigns in Southeast Asia, targeting school-aged children, may reduce overall respiratory virus circulation and improve population immunity against influenza A strains. While H5N1 is antigenically distinct from seasonal influenza, increased immune system activation, reduced co-circulation of other respiratory viruses, and lower background transmission could indirectly reduce opportunities for H5N1 to spread in humans if spillover occurs. This could modestly reduce human_contact_intensity by decreasing the baseline level of respiratory transmission in high-contact settings like schools. Additionally, such vaccination campaigns may be accompanied by heightened public health awareness and infrastructure use (e.g., clinics, monitoring), potentially enhancing public_health_response and viral_surveillance. These effects slightly reduce the likelihood of undetected or sustained human-to-human transmission of H5N1. However, the impact is limited because H5N1 transmission remains primarily dependent on zoonotic_pressure and viral_mutations, which are not directly affected by seasonal flu vaccination. Thus, the probability decreases only slightly from 0.32 to 0.30.

summary.question_text: Will there be any reported human-to-human transmission of highly pathogenic avian influenza H5N1 globally before 2026?
